1.synthesis:
Psalm 67 is the Psalter's most explicitly missiological text — a seven-verse poem that transforms the Aaronic priestly blessing into a missionary charter. The opening verse deliberately echoes Num 6:24–26 in nearly every phrase: 'May God be gracious to us and bless us, and may he cause his face to shine upon us' draws directly from 'The LORD bless you and keep you; the LORD make his face shine on you and be gracious to you.' The echo is not incidental: what was originally a priestly blessing pronounced over Israel is here turned into a prayer whose purpose is stated in v. 2 — 'so that your way may be known on earth, your saving power among all the nations.' Israel's blessing is instrumentalized: not for Israel's benefit alone, but so that YHWH's derekh (way) and yĕšûʿāh (saving power) may be known universally.

The refrain — 'Let the peoples praise you, O God; let all the peoples give you praise' (v. 3, v. 5) — is an imperative call to universal worship, not merely a prediction. The three stanzas (vv. 1–3, vv. 4–5, vv. 6–7) move from blessing-for-mission to universal judgment-and-guidance to harvest abundance as evidence of divine blessing. The harvest of v. 6 — 'The earth has yielded its increase' — simultaneously anticipates the harvest festival context of the psalm's use and symbolizes the universal harvest of the nations that the mission will produce.
1.historical_context: The Aaronic blessing of Num 6:24–26 was the prescribed priestly benediction over Israel — the blessing that closed Israel's official worship. Psalm 67 takes this exclusive covenant blessing and explicitly opens it to the nations through a purpose clause: the blessing exists for the mission. The purpose-structure echoes the Abrahamic promise: 'all peoples on earth will be blessed through you' (Gen 12:3). YHWH's 'judgment with equity' (v. 4) — yišpōt ʿammîm mîšōr (judges peoples with uprightness) — is the ground for universal praise: the nations are invited to worship not a merely tribal deity but the judge who rules all peoples with fairness. The refrain was likely sung by the assembled congregation as liturgical response between the leader's verses, giving the psalm its call-and-response character.
1.literary_structure: The psalm's seven verses divide into three stanzas with the refrain as the border between them: stanza 1 (vv. 1–2: blessing for mission), refrain (v. 3), stanza 2 (v. 4: reason for praise — universal judgment and guidance), refrain (v. 5), stanza 3 (vv. 6–7: harvest evidence of blessing + final universalism). The numerical completeness of seven verses, with the refrain at positions 3 and 5 (framing the central verse 4), may reflect intentional literary architecture. The final verse — 'God shall bless us; let all the ends of the earth fear him' — is the psalm's summary: divine blessing of Israel produces universal reverential worship from the ends of the earth.
1.theological_application: Psalm 67 provides the theological rationale for the church's mission: the covenant people are blessed for a purpose, and that purpose is universal. The 'face shining' of divine favor is not a private benefit but the source of light that illuminates YHWH's way among all the nations. This purpose-structure prevents the covenant community from treating divine blessing as an end in itself: every act of grace received is simultaneously an obligation to proclamation. Paul applies the Abrahamic version of this same logic in Gal 3:8–9, 14 — the blessing of Abraham flows to the nations through Christ, making the church Abraham's heirs and therefore carriers of the same missionary blessing.
1.christology: The psalm's purpose-clause theology — blessing given so that YHWH's way may be known among the nations — is the OT structure of the NT's Great Commission (Matt 28:18–20). The 'all authority' given to Christ is the ground; the 'all nations' is the target; the 'teaching them to observe all that I have commanded' is the mechanism. The harvest of v. 6 anticipates the harvest theology of Jesus's mission (Matt 9:37–38) and the eschatological harvest of Rev 14:14–16. The judge who guides the nations with equity (v. 4) is the one to whom judgment has been given — the Son to whom the Father has committed all judgment (John 5:22).
